cards.text:
To create a partial specialization for pointer types: {{c1::template <typename T>}}
{{c2::class Storage<T*> { ... };}}

cards.front: Why would you create a partial specialization specifically for pointer types?
cards.back:
To handle pointer-specific concerns like:
  • Deep copying (copy pointed-to value, not just pointer)
  • Memory management
  • NULL/nullptr validation
  • Avoiding dangling pointers

cards.question: What types match this specialization?
cards.answer: Any pointer type: int*, double*, char*, std::string*, MyClass*, etc.
cards.explanation: The T* pattern matches when the template argument is a pointer to any type T.

When storing pointers, you often want to copy the pointed-to value (deep copy) rather than just the pointer (shallow copy). This prevents:
  • Multiple objects owning the same memory
  • Dangling pointers when original is deleted
  • Unexpected sharing of data
examples: // Shallow: m_value = value; // Just copy pointer
// Deep: m_value = new T(*value); // Copy pointed-to object

cards.text: For Storage<int*>, the compiler will use {{c1::the pointer specialization (Storage<T*>)}} instead of {{c2::the primary template (Storage<T>)}}.

cards.front: If you have Storage<int**> (pointer to pointer), which template is used?
cards.back: The pointer specialization Storage<T*> is used, where T = int*.

cards.question: What memory management issue does this pointer specialization solve?
cards.answer: It performs deep copying and automatic cleanup. The constructor copies the pointed-to value, and the destructor deletes it.
cards.explanation: This prevents dangling pointers and ensures the Storage owns its data. Without this, deleting the original pointer would invalidate Storage's pointer.

cards.front: What happens if you pass a null pointer to Storage<T*> that dereferences it?
cards.back: Undefined behavior / crash when trying to dereference the null pointer (e.g., new T(*value) where value is null).

cards.text: A pointer specialization can provide {{c1::different member functions}} or {{c2::additional safety checks}} compared to the primary template.

cards.front: In modern C++, what is a better alternative to manually managing pointers in template classes?
cards.back: Use smart pointers (std::unique_ptr, std::shared_ptr) which handle memory management automatically.

cards.text: A pointer specialization typically performs {{c1::deep copying}} to copy the {{c2::pointed-to value}}, not just the pointer.
For Storage<int**>, the pointer specialization is used where T = {{c1::int*}}.
